content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 20, 2006


Robert F. Epstein
City Attorney
Ragghianti Freitas, LLP
874 Fourth Street, Suite D
San Rafael, California 94901-3246

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-166

Dear Mr. Epstein:

This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance on behalf of
Councilmember Cromwell regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your request is general in nature, we provide
you with informal assistance.²

QUESTION

What economic interests does Councilmember Cromwell have as a result of his
wife's business that may lead to a potential conflict of interest under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Councilmember Cromwell has economic interests in his wife's real estate clients,
the real estate brokerage firm where she is an independent contractor and the broker of
the firm as sources of income to him. He may not participate in city council decisions
that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of his sources of
income.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-06-166
Page No. 2

FACTS

Thomas Cromwell is a member of the Belvedere City Council. Councilmember
Cromwell's wife is a licensed real estate agent who works as an independent contractor
affiliated with Frank Howard Allan Realty ("Allan Realty"). She represents buyers and
sellers of residential real estate in Belvedere and throughout Marin County.

Belvedere has a total population of 2,125, is less than one-square mile in size, and
is completely built out with approximately 950 single family residences and
approximately 100 rental units. The city has only one small commercial strip and is 99
percent residential. According to the multiple listing service, during the 12 months prior
to August 15, 2006, 35 single family residential properties were sold in Belvedere. An
Allan Realty agent represented a buyer and/or seller in 15 of those 35 sales.
Ms. Cromwell represented sellers in two of those 15 sales and represented no buyers.

Ms. Cromwell has no ownership interest in Allan Realty. Like the other Allan
Realty agents, in consideration for sharing a portion of her commission income with
Allan Realty, she receives a desk, telephone and office, as well as intangible benefits of
affiliation with a realty office, including the ability to conduct business under Allan
Realty's broker license.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists
whenever a public official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests.

Step One. Is Councilmember Cromwell a "public official" within the
meaning of section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As a member of the city council of the

File No. I-06-166
Page No. 3

City of Belvedere, a local government agency, Councilmember Cromwell is a public
official.³

Step Two. Will Councilmember Cromwell be making, participating in
making or influencing a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

When City Councilmember Cromwell votes on city council decisions, he will be
making governmental decisions.

Step Three. What are the Councilmember's economic interests?

The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are described in
section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of economic interests:

1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a).)

2. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b).)

3. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2.)

³ When a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conflict of interest in
a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the
economic interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply.

File No. I-06-166
Page No. 4

4. An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more received within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3.)

5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
regulation 18703.4.)

6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is
commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.5.)

Because your question addresses only Councilmember Cromwell's potential
economic interests in his wife's employment, our analysis is limited to those interests.

Sources of Income

Income includes the community property interest in the income of a spouse.
(Section 82030.) Therefore, Councilmember Cromwell has an economic interest in any
income his wife receives or is promised. If Councilmember Cromwell's spouse has
received $1,000 or more⁴ from her real estate sales work within 12 months prior to
Councilmember Cromwell making a relevant governmental decision, her income will be
a source of income to him.

Regarding real estate commission income, the Commission regards multiple
persons as sources of income of a single payment. Regulation 18703.3(c)(2)(C) provides
the special rules for determining who are sources of commission income earned in a
given sales transaction. The following are deemed to be sources of income to a real estate
agent within the meaning of Section 87103(c) when a real estate agent receives
commission income from the result of a specific sale or similar transaction: (i) The
broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the agent works; (ii) The
person the agent represents in the transaction; and (iii) Any person who receives a
finder's or other referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to the broker, or who
makes a referral pursuant to a contract with the broker.

Also, sources of "promised" income are economic interests. (Section 87103(c).)
Commission income is deemed promised when the sale is pending (i.e., the sale is in
escrow). (Adopted Advice Letter, No. I-02-304.) Once an escrow has closed, and title
has been transferred, Councilmember Cromwell would still have an economic interest in

⁴ Income to the spouse would be $1,000 in order for the councilmember's community property
share to reach the $500 threshold of section 87103(c).

File No. I-06-166
Page No. 5

his source of income for the commission received on the sale of property. (Ibid.; Whittier
Advice Letter, No. A-00-164.)

Under the facts you have presented, Councilmember Cromwell has an economic
interest in Allan Realty, the broker of Allan Realty and any person receiving a finder's
fee, assuming his spouse receives $1,000 or more in the twelve-month period prior to the
governmental decision, for commission income she receives as a sales agent for the sale
and purchase of real property. Councilmember Cromwell will also have an economic
interest in any person that Mrs. Cromwell represents in a sales transaction, again
assuming she receives a commission of $1,000 or more.

Whether or not the above-described economic interests would present a conflict
of interest for Councilmember Cromwell in participating in a governmental decision
depends on the degree of involvement and the corresponding material effects of the
governmental decision on that economic interest. Because you have not provided facts
regarding any specific governmental decisions, we are unable to provide further analysis.

Should you have a question regarding a specific governmental decision, we
suggest you write again. We are enclosing for Councilmember Cromwell's information a
copy of regulations 18703.3, a fact-sheet regarding conflicts of interest and two advice
letters involving real estate salespersons and governmental decisions. (Randolph Advice
Letter, supra and Indelicato Advice Letter, No. A-04-104.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What economic interests does Councilmember Cromwell have as a result of his
wife's business that may lead to a potential conflict of interest under the Act?

CONCLUSION: Councilmember Cromwell has economic interests in his wife's real estate clients,
the real estate brokerage firm where she is an independent contractor and the broker of
the firm as sources of income to him. He may not participate in city council decisions
that have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of nir sources of
income.
I Gorre.nm"rt Code sections 8 1000 - g1}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 1 8329 (c)(3), copy enclosed. )
